title: Percentiles, quartiles and ranks
intro: Mean and median are about the center. But a pizzeria customer does not care about the center: "how long if I am unlucky?" How long the unluckiest 5% of orders wait, what the top 10% earn, what counts as normal — that is the language of percentiles, one of the most practical for an analyst.

beats.text: A percentile is the value below which a given share of the data lies. Move the p slider: the shaded part of the histogram is the p% of values to the left, and the yellow line is the percentile value itself. p50 is the median: half the values are below. p90 is the threshold below which 90% of the data sits — above it, the top 10%.

Quartiles are percentiles that cut the data into quarters: Q1 = p25, Q2 = p50 (the median), Q3 = p75. Between Q1 and Q3 lies the middle half of the observations; its width is the interquartile range IQR = Q3 − Q1. These anchor percentiles are shown as numbers under the chart.
Why are percentiles better than the mean on "tailed" data?
beats.predict: The data here is right-skewed. Where will p50 (the median) sit relative to the mean — to the left or to the right?
beats.reveal: To the left: the long right tail drags the mean up, while the median and the quartiles stay with the bulk — they depend on ORDER, not size. That is why incomes, response times and durations are described with percentiles: robust to outliers, honest about both the typical and the extreme.

beats.text: Slide to p95 and p99. That is "how bad it gets for the unluckiest 5% and 1%". The mean response time can look great while p99 is terrible — and p99 is what unhappy users feel. The mean hides it; the tail percentile exposes it. That is how SLAs are written: "95% of requests faster than X".

A rank is a value's "place" in the sorted row, and a percentile is a rank expressed in percent. "Your result is in the 80th percentile" means "you are above 80% of everyone else". Ranks also power non-parametric methods (like Mann–Whitney): they look at order rather than raw numbers, which makes them robust to outliers.

definitions.text: the value below which a given share of the data lies (p90 — 90% below it).
definitions.simple: p90 means you beat 90% of the data.

definitions.text: the 25/50/75 percentiles: they cut the data into quarters. Q2 is the median.
definitions.simple: the row cut into four equal parts.

definitions.text: the interquartile range — the width of the middle half of the data; a robust measure of spread.
definitions.simple: the distance from Q1 to Q3 — home of the central half.

definitions.text: a value's position in the sorted row; a percentile is a rank in percent.
definitions.simple: your number in the sorted list.

practice: Percentiles are the language of SLAs and monitoring: "95% of requests under 200 ms" is about p95, not the mean. Reliability teams barely look at the average response time precisely because tails inflate or hide it.
In product analytics percentiles describe the user distribution: the median receipt, the top 10% by revenue (where the profit usually sits), the bottom 25% by activity (churn candidates).
realLife: Children's growth charts are percentile curves: a child at the 3rd percentile is small, at the 97th — large. The doctor reads the percentile, not the absolute number.
Salary surveys publish market p25/p50/p75: you see the range instead of a deceptive "industry average" inflated by the top earners.
